T1110.003.whatAttackerDoes: The attacker tries a single common password against many user accounts to avoid lockout thresholds. Distributes failures across the user base instead of hammering one account.
T1110.003.whyItMatters: Bypasses account-lockout policies. One valid credential is enough for initial foothold, and the noise hides in normal failure rates.
commonIndicators: Low failure count per account but failures fanned out across many users
Failures from a single IP to many UPNs in a short window
Failures against accounts that don't exist (AADSTS50034)
Mixed result codes — invalid user, bad password, no MFA enrolled
T1110.003.sentinelKql:
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1h)
| where ResultType != "0"
| summarize FailCount=count(), DistinctUsers=dcount(UserPrincipalName) by IPAddress
| where DistinctUsers > 5
| sort by FailCount desc
T1110.003.elkQuery: event.category: authentication AND event.outcome: failure | stats count, dc(user.name) by source.ip
T1110.003.nextMitreStep: T1078 — Valid Accounts (if any spray hits succeed, the attacker now signs in legitimately)
T1110.003.differencesFromBruteForce: Brute Force (T1110.001) = many passwords against one account. Password Spray = one password against many accounts. Spray is harder to detect because per-account failure count stays low.

T1090.003.whatAttackerDoes: Routes traffic through multiple intermediate proxies (commonly Tor) so the apparent source IP cannot be tied back to the attacker.
T1090.003.whyItMatters: Geolocation and reputation lookups on the visible IP are useless. Forces analysts to rely on behaviour rather than IP allowlists.
commonIndicators: Source IP appears on a Tor-exit feed
Sequential access to many resources from the same Tor IP
User agents matching Tor Browser defaults
No interactive MFA event recorded for the session
T1090.003.sentinelKql:
SigninLogs
| where IPAddress in ("<TOR_IP_LIST>")
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, IPAddress, AppDisplayName, ResultType
T1090.003.differencesFromBruteForce: Not a brute-force technique — this is a network-routing technique used alongside others to anonymise sources.

T1078.whatAttackerDoes: Uses legitimate credentials (compromised via phishing, spray, theft, or insider) to access systems. The activity looks like a normal sign-in.
T1078.whyItMatters: Blends with legitimate traffic. The only signal is behavioural: unusual time, location, app, or volume.
commonIndicators: Sign-in from a geography or device the user has never used
Atypical travel — impossible distance between sequential sign-ins
Sign-in followed by atypical actions (mass download, role assignment, mailbox access)
Successful sign-in immediately after a spray / brute-force pattern
T1078.sentinelKql:
SigninLogs
| where UserPrincipalName == "<USER>"
| where TimeGenerated > ago(24h)
| summarize Countries=make_set(Location), Cities=make_set(City), IPs=make_set(IPAddress)
T1078.nextMitreStep: T1098 — Account Manipulation, or T1114 — Email Collection
T1078.differencesFromBruteForce: Brute Force tries to obtain credentials. Valid Accounts uses them. Spray is detection-by-pattern; Valid Accounts is detection-by-anomaly.

T1621.whatAttackerDoes: Already possesses the password and repeatedly triggers MFA push prompts hoping the user eventually approves one out of fatigue or confusion ("MFA bombing").
T1621.whyItMatters: Defeats MFA entirely if the user taps Approve. Often runs in the early hours when users are tired.
commonIndicators: Many push notifications to a single user in minutes
Final 'Approve' after dozens of denies
Source IP is a new geography or anonymiser
Timing in unusual hours (e.g. 02:30 local)
T1621.sentinelKql:
SigninLogs
| where UserPrincipalName == "<USER>"
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1h)
| summarize Prompts=count(), Successes=countif(ResultType == "0") by bin(TimeGenerated, 1m)
T1621.elkQuery: user.name: <USER> AND event.action: mfa_prompt | stats count by @timestamp
T1621.nextMitreStep: T1078 — Valid Accounts (the bomb succeeds → attacker is in)
T1621.differencesFromBruteForce: Brute Force targets the password. MFA Bombing targets the second factor by exploiting user behaviour.

T1566.001.whatAttackerDoes: Sends a targeted email containing a malicious attachment (Office macro, ZIP, LNK, HTML smuggling) to a specific user.
T1566.001.whyItMatters: Most common initial-access vector. One click can lead to full-host compromise.
commonIndicators: Sender domain is a lookalike of a real partner (cont0so.com)
Attachment with macro-enabled extension (.docm, .xlsm)
Office process spawning a scripting engine after the user opens the attachment
Tenant-wide delivery of the same attachment to multiple recipients
T1566.001.sentinelKql:
EmailEvents
| where Attachment == "<ATTACHMENT>"
| where Operation == "EmailDelivered"
| project TimeGenerated, SenderFromAddress, RecipientEmailAddress, Subject
T1566.001.nextMitreStep: T1204.002 — User Execution: Malicious File, then T1059 execution
T1566.001.differencesFromBruteForce: Not credential-related. This is the entry vector before any credential or execution technique.

T1105.whatAttackerDoes: Pulls additional tools/payloads onto the compromised host, typically using LOLBins (certutil, bitsadmin, curl, PowerShell) so the action blends with admin activity.
T1105.whyItMatters: Confirms an active attacker who is staging more capability — not just a one-off detonation.
commonIndicators: certutil -urlcache -split -f <url>
powershell.exe IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString(...)
bitsadmin /transfer ... http://...
Outbound HTTP from a non-browser process
T1105.sentinelKql:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where FileName in~ ("certutil.exe", "bitsadmin.exe", "curl.exe", "powershell.exe")
| where ProcessCommandLine has_any ("-urlcache", "DownloadString", "DownloadFile", "/transfer")
T1105.rapid7Query: where(process_name, IN, certutil, bitsadmin, powershell) where(command_line, CONTAINS, http)
T1105.nextMitreStep: T1059 — Command and Scripting Interpreter (executes the downloaded payload)
T1105.differencesFromBruteForce: Not credential-related. This is post-execution stager activity.

T1003.001.whatAttackerDoes: Reads LSASS process memory (where Windows stores authentication secrets) to extract cleartext passwords, NTLM hashes, and Kerberos tickets.
T1003.001.whyItMatters: Single biggest leverage step — turns one compromised host into many. Credentials extracted here are used for lateral movement.
commonIndicators: rundll32 + comsvcs.dll MiniDump targeting lsass
procdump.exe -ma lsass.exe ...
Memory access events from non-security processes to LSASS PID
Output dump file written to a public/temp folder
T1003.001.sentinelKql:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where ProcessCommandLine has_any ("lsass", "comsvcs", "MiniDump", "procdump")
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, FileName, ProcessCommandLine
T1003.001.nextMitreStep: T1550 — Use Alternate Authentication Material (pass-the-hash, pass-the-ticket)
T1003.001.differencesFromBruteForce: Brute Force guesses passwords externally. LSASS Dumping steals them directly from memory of an already-compromised host.

T1055.002.whatAttackerDoes: Writes malicious PE code into the address space of a legitimate process (typically explorer.exe / svchost.exe) and starts a remote thread to execute it.
T1055.002.whyItMatters: The malicious behaviour now runs under a trusted process, evading allowlists and many EDR heuristics.
commonIndicators: Sysmon EID 8 — CreateRemoteThread from one process to another
Source process is a recent PS / unsigned binary; target is a system process
VirtualAllocEx / WriteProcessMemory API calls observed by EDR
T1055.002.sentinelKql:
SysmonEvent
| where EventID == 8
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, SourceImage, TargetImage
T1055.002.rapid7Query: where(event_type, remote_thread) where(target_process, IN, explorer.exe, svchost.exe)
T1055.002.nextMitreStep: T1071 — Application Layer Protocol (the injected code beacons out)
T1055.002.differencesFromBruteForce: Unrelated to credentials. This is a stealth/evasion technique for code that already has execution.

T1055.whatAttackerDoes: Generic category: any technique that runs attacker code inside the address space of another process. Includes named-pipe creation for IPC between injector and payload (a Cobalt Strike calling card).
T1055.whyItMatters: Hides malicious activity inside trusted processes; reduces the surface area defenders can monitor.
commonIndicators: Named pipes matching offensive-tool defaults (msagent_*, postex_*)
Unusual parent-child relationships (rundll32 → notepad)
Modules loaded into a process that aren't on its baseline
T1055.sentinelKql:
SysmonEvent
| where EventID == 17
| where PipeName startswith "\\msagent_" or PipeName startswith "\\postex_"
T1055.rapid7Query: where(pipe_name, STARTSWITH, msagent_) OR where(pipe_name, STARTSWITH, postex_)
T1055.nextMitreStep: T1071 — Application Layer Protocol (C2 beacons through the injected code)
T1055.differencesFromBruteForce: Unrelated to credentials. Stealth technique, not access technique.

T1547.001.technique: Boot/Logon Autostart: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder
T1547.001.whatAttackerDoes: Writes a value to HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run (or equivalent) so a chosen binary executes at logon.
T1547.001.whyItMatters: Survives reboots. Most common persistence mechanism for commodity malware.
commonIndicators: Sysmon EID 13 with TargetObject ending in \Run\<name>
Run key value pointing to a Public / Temp / AppData binary
Multiple Run keys created in close succession alongside other persistence (scheduled task, WMI)
T1547.001.sentinelKql:
SysmonEvent
| where EventID == 13
| where TargetObject has "\\Run\\"
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, TargetObject, Details
T1547.001.nextMitreStep: T1059 — execution of the persisted binary at next logon
T1547.001.differencesFromBruteForce: Persistence technique — establishes long-term access, not initial access or credential theft.

T1562.001.technique: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools
T1562.001.whatAttackerDoes: Stops or disables security tooling (Sysmon, Defender, EDR agents) before performing further actions, blinding defenders.
T1562.001.whyItMatters: Often the immediate precursor to destructive activity. The 'silence' is itself a high-fidelity signal.
commonIndicators: Service stop events for Sysmon64, WinDefend, EDR agents
sc.exe stop <agent> command line
Tamper-protection events from the EDR itself
Stop followed within minutes by other suspicious activity
T1562.001.sentinelKql:
SysmonEvent
| where EventID == 7036
| where ParameterXml has_any ("Sysmon64", "WinDefend", "Sense")
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, ParameterXml
T1562.001.rapid7Query: where(service_name, IN, Sysmon64, WinDefend, MsSense) where(state, stopped)
T1562.001.nextMitreStep: T1486 — Data Encrypted for Impact (ransomware), or T1003 (credential dumping)
T1562.001.differencesFromBruteForce: Defense-evasion technique. Adjacent to credential theft / ransomware preparation rather than a credential technique itself.

T1490.whatAttackerDoes: Deletes Volume Shadow Copies, Windows Backup catalogues, or Recycle Bin contents so victims cannot restore from local snapshots.
T1490.whyItMatters: Highest-fidelity pre-ransomware indicator. By the time you see this, encryption is seconds away.
commonIndicators: vssadmin.exe delete shadows /all /quiet
wmic shadowcopy delete
wbadmin delete catalog -quiet
Same command on multiple hosts within the same minute
T1490.sentinelKql:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where ProcessCommandLine has "vssadmin" and ProcessCommandLine has "delete shadows"
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, AccountName, ProcessCommandLine
T1490.rapid7Query: where(command_line, CONTAINS, vssadmin) where(command_line, CONTAINS, delete shadows)
T1490.nextMitreStep: T1486 — Data Encrypted for Impact (the ransomware encryption phase)
T1490.differencesFromBruteForce: Impact technique, not access. Treat as imminent ransomware — switch to containment over investigation.

T1087.whatAttackerDoes: Enumerates accounts (local, domain, cloud) to plan privilege escalation and lateral movement. Tools include SharpHound, ADFind, net.exe.
T1087.whyItMatters: Confirms an attacker is planning their next move. Output files often contain everything they need to escalate.
commonIndicators: SharpHound.exe execution with -c All
net group "Domain Admins" /domain
Bulk LDAP queries from a non-administrative host
ADFind.exe -f "(objectClass=user)" ...
T1087.sentinelKql:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where FileName has_any ("SharpHound", "ADFind") or ProcessCommandLine has "Invoke-BloodHound"
T1087.nextMitreStep: T1078 — Valid Accounts, or T1550 — Use Alternate Authentication Material
T1087.differencesFromBruteForce: Reconnaissance technique. Sits before lateral movement, not before initial access.

T1204.whatAttackerDoes: Relies on a user to perform an action (open an attachment, click a link, run a binary) that runs the attacker's code.
T1204.whyItMatters: Last step of phishing. The technical detection happens earlier (email / network); the actual execution is what causes the host compromise.
commonIndicators: Process creation events triggered by user action (Office spawn)
Untrusted binaries executed from Downloads / Desktop
EICAR / test files detected for genuine controlled tests
T1204.sentinelKql:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where InitiatingProcessFileName in~ ("explorer.exe", "WINWORD.EXE", "OUTLOOK.EXE")
| where FolderPath has "\\Downloads\\" or FolderPath has "\\Desktop\\"
T1204.rapid7Query: where(initiating_process, IN, explorer.exe, WINWORD.EXE) where(folder_path, CONTAINS, Downloads)
T1204.elkQuery: process.parent.name: (explorer.exe OR WINWORD.EXE) AND file.path: (*Downloads* OR *Desktop*)
T1204.nextMitreStep: T1059 — Command and Scripting Interpreter (the executed code runs)
T1204.differencesFromBruteForce: Different category entirely — relies on human action rather than automated cracking.

T1114.003.whatAttackerDoes: Creates an inbox rule that auto-forwards (or copies) mail to an external address, exfiltrating future correspondence without further access to the mailbox.
T1114.003.whyItMatters: Persistent, low-noise data exfil. Survives password resets if not explicitly removed.
commonIndicators: Inbox rule with single-character name (.,  ) — hides in the UI
Rule ForwardTo address is external / free webmail
SubjectContainsWords with finance terms (invoice, wire, payment)
Rule created from a Tor / anonymiser source IP
T1114.003.sentinelKql:
OfficeActivity
| where Operation == "New-InboxRule"
| where Parameters has_any ("ForwardTo", "RedirectTo")
| project TimeGenerated, UserId, ClientIP, Parameters
T1114.003.nextMitreStep: T1567 — Exfiltration Over Web Service (continued mail exfiltration)
T1114.003.differencesFromBruteForce: Collection technique. Comes after initial access; designed for long-term passive theft.

T1485.whatAttackerDoes: Deliberately deletes data — files in cloud storage, OneDrive items, database tables — to cause operational damage.
T1485.whyItMatters: May be the attacker's goal (sabotage) or a cover-up of other activity. Recovery windows close fast.
commonIndicators: Mass FileDeleted operations from a single user in minutes
Cloud-side bulk delete via PowerShell / Graph API
Deletion of audit logs in the same window
T1485.sentinelKql:
OfficeActivity
| where Operation == "FileDeleted"
| summarize Count=count() by UserId, bin(TimeGenerated, 5m)
| where Count > 100
T1485.elkQuery: event.action: FileDeleted | stats count by user.name, @timestamp
T1485.nextMitreStep: Often follows T1078 / T1098 (account compromise) and precedes T1486 (encryption) or stand-alone destruction.
T1485.differencesFromBruteForce: Impact technique — focus on stopping the loss and engaging backup teams, not on detection.

T1528.whatAttackerDoes: Tricks a user into granting OAuth consent to a malicious app, or steals refresh tokens, then uses the resulting access token. Bypasses MFA entirely.
T1528.whyItMatters: The token persists past password resets and survives session revocation if refresh tokens aren't explicitly killed.
commonIndicators: Consent to application from an unverified publisher
Granted scopes include Mail.ReadWrite / Files.Read.All / offline_access
Token issuance event without an interactive MFA event in the same flow
Application accessing mail / files from an anomalous IP
T1528.sentinelKql:
OfficeActivity
| where Operation == "Consent to application"
| project TimeGenerated, UserId, ClientIP, Parameters
T1528.nextMitreStep: T1114 — Email Collection (using the stolen token to read mail)
T1528.differencesFromBruteForce: Credential-level technique but targets tokens rather than passwords. Password reset alone does not contain it.

T1567.002.whatAttackerDoes: Uploads stolen data to a cloud storage service (Dropbox, Mega, Google Drive, or SharePoint anonymous links) instead of directly to attacker infrastructure.
T1567.002.whyItMatters: Egress traffic looks like normal cloud usage and is rarely blocked at the firewall.
commonIndicators: Anonymous SharePoint / OneDrive links created on sensitive files
Outbound traffic to cloud storage endpoints from non-baseline accounts
Single user uploading abnormal volumes in a short window
T1567.002.sentinelKql:
OfficeActivity
| where Operation == "AnonymousLinkCreated"
| project TimeGenerated, UserId, SourceFileName, ClientIP
T1567.002.nextMitreStep: Often the final attacker step — followed only by T1070 (defense evasion / log clearing).
T1567.002.differencesFromBruteForce: Exfiltration technique. Detection focuses on policy violations, not authentication failures.

T1213.whatAttackerDoes: Harvests data from internal repositories (SharePoint, Confluence, eDiscovery exports, code repos) where business-sensitive information accumulates.
T1213.whyItMatters: Compliance / legal-grade exfil potential. eDiscovery exports especially can contain entire executive mailboxes.
commonIndicators: SearchExportDownloaded by a recently-promoted admin account
Bulk SharePoint downloads from a new device
Repo cloning by an account with no engineering role
T1213.sentinelKql:
OfficeActivity
| where Operation == "SearchExportDownloaded"
| project TimeGenerated, UserId, ClientIP, Parameters

T1530.whatAttackerDoes: Reads or exposes data in cloud storage (Azure Blob, S3, GCS) — often by misconfiguring access (public containers) or using stolen keys.
T1530.whyItMatters: Cloud storage often holds backups, secrets, and PII. A single misconfiguration can constitute a breach.
commonIndicators: Storage account configuration changed to allowBlobPublicAccess
Bulk reads via a service principal from an external IP
Container ACL changes to public outside change windows
T1530.sentinelKql:
AzureActivity
| where OperationNameValue has "STORAGEACCOUNTS"
| where Properties has "publicAccess"
| project TimeGenerated, Caller, CallerIpAddress, ResourceId, Properties
T1530.rapid7Query: where(resource_type, storage_account) where(property, allowBlobPublicAccess) where(value, true)
T1530.differencesFromBruteForce: Cloud-specific data-theft technique. Detection is config-state, not auth-event.

T1078.004.whatAttackerDoes: Uses compromised cloud identities (Entra ID users, service principals, AWS IAM users) to access cloud resources — often bypassing on-prem controls.
T1078.004.whyItMatters: Cloud accounts can perform broad actions across many services with a single credential.
commonIndicators: JIT VM access requested from a non-allowlisted IP
Service principal sign-in from a country never seen before
Same cloud identity active in two geographies within minutes
T1078.004.sentinelKql:
SigninLogs
| where AppDisplayName != ""
| where UserPrincipalName contains "svc-" or UserPrincipalName contains "sp-"
| where IPAddress != "<EXPECTED_IP>"
T1078.004.elkQuery: user.type: service_principal AND source.geo.country_name: NOT (US OR UK)
T1078.004.nextMitreStep: T1098 — Account Manipulation (escalating with the cloud account)
T1078.004.differencesFromBruteForce: Subset of T1078 specialised for cloud identities. Same detection pattern as T1078 with cloud-specific log sources.

T1552.001.whatAttackerDoes: Reads secrets out of config files, environment variables, Key Vaults, or CI variables that aren't sufficiently restricted.
T1552.001.whyItMatters: Often the second hop after initial compromise — promotes a foothold into broader access (DB creds, API keys, certificates).
commonIndicators: Key Vault secret reads by an unusual identity / IP
Reading appsettings.json / .env files via remote shell
Service principals reading secrets they don't normally touch
T1552.001.sentinelKql:
AzureActivity
| where OperationNameValue has "KEYVAULT/SECRETS/READ"
| project TimeGenerated, Caller, CallerIpAddress, ResourceId, Properties
T1552.001.nextMitreStep: T1078 — Valid Accounts (using the freshly-retrieved credentials)
T1552.001.differencesFromBruteForce: Credential theft from data-at-rest rather than from network or memory.

T1133.whatAttackerDoes: Uses internet-facing remote services (RDP, SSH, VPN, exposed management consoles) — often misconfigured to allow inbound from anywhere.
T1133.whyItMatters: Single config error can directly expose internal assets to opportunistic scanning and brute force from the public internet.
commonIndicators: NSG rule allowing 0.0.0.0/0 inbound on 22, 3389, or 5985
Firewall rule audit showing 'Any' source on management port
Service exposed externally with no jump-host requirement
T1133.sentinelKql:
AzureActivity
| where OperationNameValue has "NETWORKSECURITYGROUPS/SECURITYRULES/WRITE"
| where Properties has "0.0.0.0/0"
| project TimeGenerated, Caller, Properties
T1133.nextMitreStep: T1110 — Brute Force, against the newly-exposed service
T1133.differencesFromBruteForce: Provides the surface that brute force then targets. Detection is config-side, not auth-side.

T1496.whatAttackerDoes: Steals compute or bandwidth (commonly for cryptocurrency mining) from a compromised host or cloud account.
T1496.whyItMatters: Indicates compromise even when the financial damage is low — same entry vector that hosts a miner could host anything.
commonIndicators: xmrig / nbminer / cpuminer process names
Outbound traffic to mining pool domains (pool.minexmr.com, etc)
Sustained CPU at 100% on cloud build agents
T1496.sentinelKql:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where FileName has_any ("xmrig", "cpuminer", "nbminer") or ProcessCommandLine has "pool.minexmr"
T1496.nextMitreStep: Investigate the entry vector — mining is the symptom, not the goal.
T1496.differencesFromBruteForce: Impact technique. Detection is straightforward — focus the investigation on how the miner landed.

T1041.whatAttackerDoes: Sends stolen data back over the same C2 channel used for command/control — typically HTTPS to the attacker's infrastructure.
T1041.whyItMatters: Hides exfil inside legitimate-looking outbound HTTPS sessions; volume is the main signal.
commonIndicators: Large BytesOut from a single host to a single external IP
Outbound to a TI-confirmed C2 IP
Consistent transfer size every beacon interval
T1041.sentinelKql:
AzureFirewallNetworkRule
| where DstIP == "<C2_IP>"
| summarize Bytes=sum(toint(BytesOut)) by SrcIP
| sort by Bytes desc
T1041.nextMitreStep: Final stage for many attacks — exfiltration is often the endpoint of the kill chain.
T1041.differencesFromBruteForce: Exfiltration technique. Detected by volume / destination reputation rather than auth signal.

T1071.004.whatAttackerDoes: Encodes C2 commands and exfil data inside DNS queries / responses — abusing a protocol nearly every network allows outbound.
T1071.004.whyItMatters: Often the last C2 channel left running when egress is restricted.
commonIndicators: Very long subdomain labels (data-carrying)
High query volume to a single parent domain
TXT record queries from non-resolver processes
T1071.004.sentinelKql:
DeviceNetworkEvents
| where RemotePort == 53
| where RemoteUrl contains "<TUNNEL_DOMAIN>"
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, RemoteUrl, InitiatingProcessFileName
T1071.004.nextMitreStep: T1041 — Exfiltration Over C2 Channel (via the same DNS tunnel)
T1071.004.differencesFromBruteForce: Command-and-control technique. Detected by query volume and label length, not auth.

T1071.001.whatAttackerDoes: C2 over HTTP/HTTPS — blends with normal web traffic. Beacons at a regular interval to fetch tasks.
T1071.001.whyItMatters: Most common C2 channel for commodity and APT alike. Periodicity is the main behavioural signal.
commonIndicators: Consistent interval between connections to one external IP (low jitter)
Small request size, larger response size (task pickup)
TLS to a host with self-signed or freshly-issued certificate
T1071.001.sentinelKql:
DeviceNetworkEvents
| where RemoteIP == "<C2_IP>"
| summarize Count=count() by bin(Timestamp, 5m)
T1071.001.differencesFromBruteForce: C2 / network technique. Detected via periodicity, not credential events.

T1187.whatAttackerDoes: Coerces a host or user to authenticate to an attacker-controlled SMB / UNC / WebDAV endpoint — capturing NTLM hashes for offline cracking or relay.
T1187.whyItMatters: Even a failed session leaks the hash. Outbound SMB from a workstation to the internet is the strongest signal.
commonIndicators: Outbound SMB (port 445) to public IP
HTML / RTF documents with UNC paths to external hosts
PrintNightmare-style exploitation involving network spool calls
T1187.sentinelKql:
DeviceNetworkEvents
| where RemotePort == 445
| where not(RemoteIP startswith "10.") and not(RemoteIP startswith "172.") and not(RemoteIP startswith "192.168.")
T1187.elkQuery: destination.port: 445 AND NOT destination.ip: (10.0.0.0/8 OR 172.16.0.0/12 OR 192.168.0.0/16)
T1187.nextMitreStep: T1003 — OS Credential Dumping (cracking the captured hash offline)
T1187.differencesFromBruteForce: Credential-capture technique that doesn't require interactive guessing. Strong network-side detection.

T1059.001.whatAttackerDoes: Runs PowerShell — typically with -EncodedCommand to obscure payload — to execute downloader / loader code on the host.
T1059.001.whyItMatters: Native to Windows, signed binary, allowed by default. Encoded command lines are the highest-fidelity signal.
commonIndicators: powershell.exe with -enc / -EncodedCommand / -e
Parent is an Office product (WINWORD, EXCEL, POWERPNT)
ExecutionPolicy bypass flags (-ep bypass, -nop)
Outbound network from powershell.exe to a non-corporate IP
T1059.001.sentinelKql:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where FileName == "powershell.exe"
| where ProcessCommandLine has_any ("-enc", "-EncodedCommand", "-e ")
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, ProcessCommandLine, InitiatingProcessFileName
T1059.001.nextMitreStep: T1105 — Ingress Tool Transfer (the encoded command typically downloads a stage 2)
T1059.001.differencesFromBruteForce: Execution technique. Encoded payload is the key signal — decode and analyse before anything else.

T1114.002.whatAttackerDoes: Accesses mailboxes remotely via stolen credentials or tokens (no on-host malware required). Common in BEC chains.
T1114.002.whyItMatters: Pure-cloud compromise — no endpoint signal. Detection lives in O365 audit logs and source-IP behaviour.
commonIndicators: MailItemsAccessed events from anomalous IPs (Tor, foreign geo)
Bulk session retrievals (many items in one MailItemsAccessed)
Multiple mailboxes accessed by the same source IP in sequence
T1114.002.sentinelKql:
EmailEvents
| where Operation == "MailItemsAccessed"
| where SrcIP == "<ATTACKER_IP>"
| project TimeGenerated, UserId, MailboxId, SrcIP
T1114.002.nextMitreStep: T1114.003 — Email Forwarding Rule (for persistent exfiltration)
T1114.002.differencesFromBruteForce: Collection technique. No password-guessing involved — relies on already-obtained tokens or credentials.

T1046.whatAttackerDoes: Scans the internal network to enumerate listening services, planning lateral movement to whatever responds.
T1046.whyItMatters: Internal scans from a workstation are unambiguous — workstations don't legitimately probe peers.
commonIndicators: Many destinations, many ports, narrow time window from a single source
Process invocation of nmap.exe / masscan.exe / Test-NetConnection in a loop
IDPS rule triggered on port-sweep timing
T1046.sentinelKql:
AzureFirewallNetworkRule
| where SrcIP == "<HOST_IP>"
| summarize Attempts=count(), DistinctPorts=dcount(DstPort) by SrcIP, bin(TimeGenerated, 5m)
| where DistinctPorts > 10
T1046.nextMitreStep: T1021 — Remote Services (lateral movement to whatever responded)
T1046.differencesFromBruteForce: Network reconnaissance, not credential attack. Detected by fan-out pattern.

T1486.whatAttackerDoes: Encrypts files on disk and on accessible shares to deny access; usually paired with a ransom demand.
T1486.whyItMatters: Recovery requires functional backups + clean rebuild. Speed of detection determines blast radius.
commonIndicators: Mass file rename events with consistent suffix (.LOCKD, .encrypted)
Ransom notes written to many folders
Preceded within minutes by T1490 (shadow copy deletion)
T1486.sentinelKql:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where ProcessCommandLine has_any (".LOCKD", ".encrypted", "ransom_note")
| summarize Files=count() by DeviceName, bin(Timestamp, 1m)
T1486.elkQuery: event.action: rename AND file.extension: (LOCKD OR encrypted) | stats count by host.name
T1486.nextMitreStep: Final stage — focus on containment and recovery, not further detection.
T1486.differencesFromBruteForce: Impact technique. Same handling as T1490 — switch to ransomware playbook immediately.

T1098.003.whatAttackerDoes: Adds privileged roles to an account (often a service account or a fresh user) the attacker controls, giving them persistent elevated access independent of the original entry.
T1098.003.whyItMatters: Survives password resets on the original compromised account. Service accounts are rarely monitored, making this a stealthy persistence move.
commonIndicators: ROLEASSIGNMENTS/WRITE outside the change window
Caller account itself is recently compromised (cross-reference with phishing alerts)
Grantee is a service account or a freshly-created user
Role granted is Global Administrator / Privileged Role Administrator
T1098.003.sentinelKql:
AzureActivity
| where OperationNameValue has "ROLEASSIGNMENTS/WRITE"
| project TimeGenerated, Caller, CallerIpAddress, ResourceId, Properties
T1098.003.rapid7Query: where(operation, RoleAssignment) where(role, IN, GlobalAdmin, PrivilegedRoleAdmin)
T1098.003.nextMitreStep: T1078.004 — Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts (using the newly-privileged identity)
T1098.003.differencesFromBruteForce: Persistence technique in the identity plane. Detection is event-side, not behavioural.

T1218.whatAttackerDoes: Uses legitimate, Microsoft-signed binaries (rundll32, mshta, regsvr32) to execute malicious code — bypassing application allowlists.
T1218.whyItMatters: Allowlists trust signed binaries by default. The attacker piggybacks on that trust.
commonIndicators: rundll32.exe ... ,DllRegisterServer with non-system DLLs
mshta.exe http://...
regsvr32 /s /n /u /i:http://... scrobj.dll (Squiblydoo)
T1218.sentinelKql:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where FileName in~ ("rundll32.exe", "mshta.exe", "regsvr32.exe")
| where ProcessCommandLine has_any ("http://", "https://", "scrobj.dll")
T1218.rapid7Query: where(process_name, IN, rundll32.exe, mshta.exe, regsvr32.exe) where(command_line, CONTAINS, http)
T1218.elkQuery: process.name: (rundll32.exe OR mshta.exe OR regsvr32.exe) AND process.command_line: *http*
T1218.nextMitreStep: T1059 — Command and Scripting Interpreter (the proxied payload runs)
T1218.differencesFromBruteForce: Defense evasion through trusted-binary abuse, not a credential technique.

T1053.005.whatAttackerDoes: Creates a Windows scheduled task that runs attacker code at boot, logon, or on a recurring schedule.
T1053.005.whyItMatters: Standard Windows feature so easily overlooked. Often paired with Run keys / WMI for redundancy.
commonIndicators: schtasks.exe /create from a non-admin context
Task name impersonates Microsoft (GoogleUpdaterTask, OfficeTelemetry)
Task action points to a Public / Temp / AppData binary
T1053.005.sentinelKql:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where FileName == "schtasks.exe"
| where ProcessCommandLine has "/create"
T1053.005.nextMitreStep: T1059 — execution of the persisted task action

T1546.003.technique: Event Triggered Execution: WMI Event Subscription
T1546.003.whatAttackerDoes: Registers a WMI consumer + filter pair so attacker code runs when a specific event fires (boot, logon, time-of-day).
T1546.003.whyItMatters: Stealth persistence — lives in the WMI repository, often missed by AV scanners.
commonIndicators: Sysmon EID 20 / 21 (WMI consumer/binding) outside of management tooling
ActiveScriptEventConsumer / CommandLineEventConsumer with attacker-controlled script
WMI subscription created within the same hour as other persistence mechanisms
T1546.003.sentinelKql:
SysmonEvent
| where EventID in (20, 21)
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Consumer, Destination
T1546.003.nextMitreStep: T1059 — execution when the WMI event fires
T1546.003.differencesFromBruteForce: Persistence technique, WMI subsystem rather than registry / scheduled task.
